bugs.recommended_fix: Implement mandatory qualification gate: No deal advances past discovery without confirmed meeting scheduled with economic buyer within 14 days. Train AEs on executive engagement tactics including value-based messaging, business case development, and upward navigation techniques.
bugs.expected_improvement: Increase close rate by 15-20% on deals >$40K and reduce average sales cycle by 18 days by eliminating late-stage stalls due to budget authority issues
bugs.description: AEs consistently sell to technical buyers or mid-level champions without engaging budget holders or C-level executives. Deals progress through technical validation but stall at procurement because no executive sponsor exists to justify investment.
bugs.root_cause_detail: AEs lack qualification discipline and executive engagement skills. They are comfortable selling technical value to practitioners but avoid or don't know how to navigate upward to economic buyers. No enforcement of qualification criteria requiring economic buyer access before advancing deals.
bugs.title: Never Talk to Economic Buyer
implementation_steps: Add CRM workflow rule: Deals cannot move to 'Demo' stage without 'Economic Buyer Identified' and 'Economic Buyer Meeting Scheduled' fields completed
Create 2-hour training module on 'Selling to the C-Suite' covering executive pain points, business case frameworks, and conversation scripts
Require AEs to submit executive engagement plan (who, when, what business outcomes) in weekly deal reviews for all opportunities >$40K
Build library of executive-level ROI decks and business case templates by persona (CFO, CTO, CEO)
Implement peer shadowing: Have top performers who excel at executive engagement mentor struggling AEs on 3 live calls
Add leading indicator metric to AE scorecards: '% of qualified deals with economic buyer engaged in first 21 days'

bugs.recommended_fix: Deploy ROI calculator tool and mandate customized business case presentation for all deals >$35K before pricing discussion. Train AEs on discovery questions that uncover quantifiable pain and cost of status quo.
bugs.expected_improvement: Reduce discount rate from average 18% to under 10% and increase win rate by 12% on competitive deals by establishing clear value differentiation before price discussion
bugs.description: AEs fail to build quantified business cases showing payback period, cost savings, or value metrics tied to buyer's stated goals. When price objections arise, AEs have no ammunition to defend premium pricing and immediately resort to discounting.
bugs.root_cause_detail: No standardized ROI methodology or tooling provided to AEs. Discovery calls focus on technical requirements rather than business outcomes and cost of inaction. AEs lack training on value-based selling and financial business case construction.
bugs.title: Weak ROI Justification
implementation_steps: Build interactive ROI calculator tool with industry-specific templates (by vertical: retail, healthcare, fintech, etc.) showing TCO, payback period, and 3-year value
Create discovery question framework focused on quantifying current state costs: 'How many hours per week? What's loaded cost of that time? What revenue is at risk? What's cost of errors/downtime?'
Require AEs to complete ROI model and review with manager before sending pricing proposal on deals >$35K
Add stage gate: 'Business Case Presented' must be checked before moving to 'Proposal' stage
Develop competitive battle cards showing TCO comparison vs. top 3 competitors with value gap analysis
Implement rule: No discounting allowed until ROI model has been presented to economic buyer and objection has been documented as 'budget constraint' not 'value uncertainty'

bugs.recommended_fix: Implement comprehensive technical qualification checklist in discovery stage with automated flagging for known product gaps. Create escalation protocol for deals requiring roadmap features or compliance certifications.
bugs.expected_improvement: Reduce wasted sales cycles by 25% through earlier disqualification and increase close rate by 10% by preventing late-stage technical surprises
bugs.description: AEs discover critical product limitations or compliance gaps (HIPAA, EU data residency, real-time processing) late in sales cycle after investing significant time. Requirements not thoroughly qualified in discovery, leading to wasted effort and deal collapse at technical validation stage.
bugs.root_cause_detail: Discovery call templates don't include comprehensive technical and compliance requirement checklist. AEs lack product knowledge depth to probe for potential gaps. No early warning system to flag deals with requirements that match known product limitations.
bugs.title: Product Gaps Not Qualified Early
implementation_steps: Build discovery checklist covering: compliance requirements (HIPAA, SOC2, GDPR, data residency), technical architecture constraints, integration requirements, performance/scale needs, security mandates
Create CRM automation: When specific requirements are checked (e.g., 'HIPAA required'), trigger alert to Solutions Engineering and tag deal with 'Requires Compliance Review'
Maintain living document of 'Known Product Gaps' with workarounds, roadmap timeline, and win/loss history - require AEs to review before discovery calls
Establish 48-hour SLA for Solutions Engineering to review flagged deals and provide gap analysis
Train AEs on disqualification criteria: If prospect has hard requirement for feature not on roadmap within their timeline, disqualify immediately and set re-engagement date
For enterprise deals (>$75K), require technical qualification call with Solutions Engineer before demo to validate all requirements can be met

bugs.recommended_fix: Mandate minimum 3 active contacts per deal with at least one economic buyer. Implement stakeholder mapping exercise in discovery and track multi-threading metrics in pipeline reviews.
bugs.expected_improvement: Reduce deal loss rate from champion churn/deprioritization by 30% and increase average deal size by 15% through broader organizational engagement
bugs.description: Deals remain single-threaded to one champion throughout sales cycle with no multi-threading to other stakeholders, decision makers, or influencers. When champion loses interest, changes priorities, or lacks authority, deal dies with no backup relationships.
bugs.root_cause_detail: AEs don't understand stakeholder mapping or lack confidence to request introductions to additional contacts. No accountability for relationship breadth in deal reviews. CRM doesn't track or alert on single-threaded risk.
bugs.title: Single-Thread Syndrome
implementation_steps: Add CRM validation rule: Deals >$40K cannot move past 'Discovery' stage without minimum 3 contacts associated with opportunity
Create stakeholder mapping template identifying: Economic Buyer, Technical Buyer, Champion, Legal/Procurement, End Users - require completion by end of week 2
Train AEs on requesting introductions: 'Who else should be part of this conversation? Can you introduce me to [role] so we can address their concerns early?'
Implement weekly deal review question: 'If your champion left the company tomorrow, who else could move this deal forward?'
Build email templates for requesting multi-threading: 'I'd love to get 15 minutes with your CFO/CTO/VP to ensure we're aligned on business outcomes'
Add leading indicator metric: 'Average contacts per deal' and 'Deals with economic buyer engaged' to team dashboards

bugs.recommended_fix: Implement discount approval process requiring documented value reinforcement attempt before any price concession. Train AEs on objection diagnosis framework and value-based negotiation techniques.
bugs.expected_improvement: Increase average deal margin by 8-12% and improve price positioning confidence across team, resulting in $180K+ annual revenue protection
bugs.description: When buyers raise price concerns or competitive alternatives, AEs immediately offer discounts without diagnosing root cause of objection or attempting to reinforce value. Discounting used as crutch instead of value-based negotiation.
bugs.root_cause_detail: AEs lack objection handling skills and confidence in product value. No approval process or justification required for discounting. Compensation structure may inadvertently incentivize closing at any price rather than defending margin.
bugs.title: Discount-First Response to Objections
implementation_steps: Create discount approval workflow: Any discount >10% requires manager approval with required fields: 'Objection Root Cause', 'Value Reinforcement Attempted', 'Competitive Alternative', 'Business Justification'

Train AEs on objection handling framework: Clarify (ask questions to understand real concern), Isolate (is this the only barrier?), Validate (acknowledge concern), Respond (address with value, not price)
Build objection response playbook covering top 10 objections with value-based responses and proof points
Require AEs to present ROI model and case studies before discussing any price adjustment
Implement peer review: Before offering >15% discount, AE must present deal strategy to team and get feedback on value reinforcement options
Adjust compensation: Reduce commission rate on deals with >20% discount to incentivize margin protection

bugs.recommended_fix: Implement mandatory mutual action plan (MAP) for all qualified opportunities with specific dates, stakeholders, and decision criteria agreed with buyer. Train AEs on collaborative close planning.
bugs.expected_improvement: Reduce sales cycle length by 12 days on average and decrease 'no decision' loss rate by 25% through increased buyer engagement and accountability
bugs.description: Deals progress without clear next steps, timeline commitments, or mutual accountability between buyer and seller. Vague commitments like 'I'll get back to you' or 'let me talk to my team' accepted without specific dates or deliverables, leading to stalls and ghosting.
bugs.root_cause_detail: No standardized close plan template or mutual action plan methodology taught to AEs. Deal progression tracked by AE activity rather than buyer commitments. AEs uncomfortable asking for specific commitments from prospects.
bugs.title: No Mutual Action Plan
implementation_steps: Create mutual action plan template with columns: Date, Action Item, Owner (Buyer/Seller), Success Criteria, Status
Require MAP to be built collaboratively with champion by end of discovery call and reviewed/updated after every subsequent meeting
Train AEs on positioning MAP as buyer enablement tool: 'Let's map out the steps to get you live by [target date]. What needs to happen on your side? Here's what we'll deliver...'
Add CRM field: 'Next Buyer Commitment' (date and specific action) - must be populated to keep deal in active pipeline
Implement rule: If buyer misses committed action item without rescheduling, AE must escalate to champion and/or manager within 48 hours
In weekly pipeline reviews, ask: 'What specific commitment did the buyer make? When? Who is accountable on their side?'

executive_report.executive_summary: Analysis of 10 closed deals reveals $522,000 in preventable ARR losses—70% of total closed-lost revenue. Two deals (DEAL-006, DEAL-009) were incorrectly categorized as losses and actually represent $79K in won ARR, demonstrating CRM data quality issues. The remaining 8 deals show systematic GTM execution failures: 50% never engaged an economic buyer, 40% failed to build quantified ROI cases, and 40% discovered product gaps too late in the cycle. Three deals ($204K ARR) have high win-back probability within 90 days. Six critical GTM bugs are costing us $1.5M+ annually in lost revenue and wasted sales capacity. Immediate action on economic buyer engagement and ROI methodology could recover $150K+ in Q1 2026 and prevent $400K+ in future losses.

playbook.raw_text:
{
  "battle_cards": [
    {
      "bug_id": "GTM-001",
      "bug_title": "Never Talk to Economic Buyer",
      "the_problem": "50% of our deals (5 out of 10) died because we sold to champions who couldn't sign checks. $269K in ARR lost because technical buyers loved us but had no budget authority. Average deal that skips economic buyer engagement has 35% lower close rate and stalls 23 days longer at procurement.",
      "the_fix": "Within first 14 days of qualified opportunity, you MUST have a scheduled meeting with the person who controls the budget (VP+, budget owner, or C-level). If your champion won't introduce you, they're not a real champion. No exceptions: deals don't advance to demo without this meeting booked.",
      "talk_track": "AFTER CHAMPION SHOWS INTEREST: 'Sarah, I'm glad this resonates with you. To make sure we're solving the right business problem and can move quickly, I typically involve [CFO/VP Operations/CTO] early in the conversation. They help us quantify the ROI and ensure we have budget allocated. Would Thursday or Friday work better for a 20-minute call with you and [economic buyer]?' WHEN CHAMPION HESITATES: 'I totally understand. Here's what I've learned - when we don't involve budget holders early, deals that should take 45 days end up taking 90+ days and get stuck in procurement. I'd rather find out now if the business case doesn't work than waste your time for three months. Can you at least introduce me via email so I can share our ROI framework?'",
      "discovery_questions": [
        "Who ultimately approves budget for this type of investment at your company?",
        "Walk me through your purchasing process for software in this price range - who needs to sign off?",
        "When you've bought similar tools before, who was involved in the final decision?",
        "If we build a compelling business case together, who would you present it to for approval?",
        "What's your budget authority level, and who do you report to on spending decisions?",
        "Has budget already been allocated for this initiative, or do we need to build a business case to secure it?"
      ],
      "red_flags": [
        "Champion says 'I'll handle the internal discussions' when you ask about economic buyer",
        "You're in week 3+ and still haven't met anyone with 'VP' or 'C' in their title",
        "Champion can't answer budget questions or says 'we'll figure that out later'",
        "Deal is marked 'Technical Win' but no business case discussion has happened",
        "Champion introduces you to peers at their level instead of upward to leadership",
        "You hear 'my boss doesn't need to be involved yet' more than once"
      ],
      "success_example": "DEAL-006 ($52K ARR): AE met with IT Director (champion) on Day 1, immediately asked about approval process. By Day 8, had intro call with CTO (economic buyer) where AE presented business case showing $180K annual savings from automation. CTO became active sponsor, allocated budget from Q3 planning, deal closed in 38 days at full price. Key moment: AE said 'I don't want to waste your director's time if this isn't a priority for you' - CTO appreciated directness and fast-tracked the decision.",
      "metrics_to_track": [
        "% of deals >$40K with economic buyer meeting scheduled within 14 days (target: 100%)",
        "Average days to first economic buyer engagement (target: <10 days)",
        "Close rate on deals with economic buyer engaged in first 21 days vs. after day 21",
        "% of lost deals where economic buyer was never identified (target: 0%)"
      ],
      "email_template": "Subject: Quick question about decision process\n\nHi [Champion],\n\nReally enjoyed our conversation about [specific pain point]. Based on what you shared, I think we can deliver [specific outcome] within [timeframe].\n\nQuick question: When you've invested in tools like this before, who typically needs to be part of the evaluation? I want to make sure we're addressing the right business priorities and can move efficiently.\n\nWould it make sense to get 15 minutes with you and [CFO/CTO/VP] next week? I can share our ROI framework and make sure this aligns with broader company goals.\n\nWhat does your Thursday afternoon look like?\n\n[Your name]",
      "call_script_snippet": "DISCOVERY CALL (Minute 18-20): 'Before we talk next steps, help me understand your decision process. If we build something you love, what happens next? [LISTEN] Got it. And who controls the budget for this? [LISTEN] Perfect. Here's what I've learned - when we involve [economic buyer role] early, we can usually get to a decision in 4-6 weeks. When we wait, it drags to 3+ months because we're building the business case from scratch at the end. Would it make sense to get 20 minutes with you and [name] next week so I can understand their priorities too?'"
    },
